speaker: interviewer
text: --1995. Survivor, Alexander Kronhart, reel 2.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So as I said, I moved to Brooklyn, Eastern Parkway, near the Botanical Gardens. And then one day, I decided I'll go to the Democratic Club, 'cause in New York, in Manhattan, I was a member of the Democratic Club on Second Avenue and 8th Street.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one evening, I took a walk. And I found the Democratic Club. And McCooey was the-- the leader at that time, John McCooey.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I walk into the room. And lo and behold, I see this Rebecca. Of course, I didn't know her name then, but she looks familiar to me. I said-- I walked over to her and I said, I saw you on the train. You were handing out sandwiches. And she didn't remember. We got to talking, and we found that we were both lawyers. She had been admitted to the bar, too. And talking, and we had about the same cases. We handled criminal cases. And so I walked her home. She lived on Union Street, a big stoop. And I walked her home. And her brother, her older brother, was a doctor. He lived there with his wife and the children. And so then I made a date. I said, I'll call you and we'll go out sometime, which I did. We went to the movies from time to time. And to make a long story short, one year later we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were going to elope. But her brother found out, the doctor. He said, you're going to elope? You may as well get married. I'll arrange for the wedding. So he went to the temple and he got the rabbi. And on August 31st, 1932-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] sorry. Correction. 1933. August 31st 1933, we got married. And I remember she told the rabbi, if you take more than five minutes I'm going to walk out. So we got married. And then we were both busy with cases. And we took a short trip to Albany and then back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was in her own office at 51 Chambers Street. And I was at 299 Broadway, just a couple blocks away. And we got together several times. And I sent-- I went to the bookstore. I sent her some books that I was reading that I liked, I gave her. And I sent flowers. And then eventually, she moved into my office and we were doing law business, Kronhart Kronhart. And she had some very important cases, too. Very important. And she also was a labor lawyer. And then we moved to 80 Wall Street in the Wall Street district, right down the street from the Treasury Department and the stock market.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1938. No, '37. '38, '39, '40. '40. In '42, when the war started, one morning she came into the office and said, Al, there's a war going on. I'm going to Washington. So I said, OK. You go to Washington. We had some cases to try, but I tried them. She went to Washington. And Wayne Morse-- he later became a senator-- he was the head of the National War Labor Board. And he hired her right on the spot.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was the senator from Alaska later.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He became a senator later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Later, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oregon.

speaker: interviewee
text: A very-- I met him--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oregon. Oregon, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I met him too. We went to lunch, my wife [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and the-- the one that headed the War Labor Board. We went to lunch. And then, when at first I visited, she was sitting with the BOARD every night. They were so busy. John Lewis--

speaker: interviewer
text: John L. Lewis.

speaker: interviewee
text: John L. Lewis, yeah. He was the iron-- the head of the iron union. And the purpose of the National War Labor Board was to prevent strikes during the war. And as I said, I came down, and I hardly could see my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was basically a counsel, then, to the War Labor Board.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And then one friend from New York who also worked in Washington, he saw me at lunch one day. And he said, Al, we could use you here, too, with your background. So I went to the OPA, that's the Office of Price Administration. And there, they hired me as a liaison to the War Department and the Pentagon on transportation matters. And my first assignment was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hand away from your--

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: I want you to keep your hand away from the microphone, because it's making noise.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. So my first assignment, they found that Henry Ford was overcharging the government a million dollars for transportation of disassembled warplanes to various parts of Europe. And that was my first assignment. So I had to go to Dearborn, Michigan to examine their books. And when I got to Dearborn, I was surprised. I saw a lot of soldiers there, you know, and tents. You know, Henry Ford was a pacifist. And he wasn't very favorable to the Americans. He wanted Germany to win the war, you know. He was anti-Semite too. And when I-- the soldiers told me that they were stationed there. Ostensibly, the government took over his plant, you know. But they let him run it. And then I got in and I met with Henry Ford. And I told my mission, that I'm going to examine his books. I stayed over. And then I had breakfast with him in his private dining room. And then I examined the books. And I got back a half a million dollars for the government.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your impression of Henry Ford?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was very stoic and very independent. And, well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: He must have known. I didn't tell him. He didn't ask. But he was an anti-Semite, I can tell you that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we know that, because he-- he actually promoted the distribution of the Zion.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. So then I came back to Washington. And then one day with my wife I visit the Mellon Museum. And who do I see there, Mickey Marcus, Dave Marcus, with his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, what are you doing here? I told him all about my wife and that I am now with the OPA and I examined Henry Ford's books, and so on. And he said, how would you like to be a judge in the Nuremberg trials?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we lost a nephew in the Battle of the Bulge, so I wasn't too happy about going to Germany. And-- but then he said, well, think about it. I'll have you meet General Clay and he can appoint you. And-- and then he said, if you want to go to Germany, I'll send you to Japan. And Japan was 6,000 miles away. My wife wouldn't fly, so she wouldn't go. And then again, we were still pretty much upset about the loss of our nephew, so we said forget it. But then a week later I said, look, it could be a good experience. Let's go. So he introduced me to General Clay. And General Clay wrote an order for me to be a judge.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went over. And then my wife wanted to work in the-- the same theater of operations. But government policy, State Department, says husband and wife couldn't work in the same theater, so they wanted her to go to Japan. She wouldn't go.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, I'd rather go with my husband. So she came over, too. And what she went-- did was to go to the DP camps and help the DP people fill out their applications for-- to go to the US, you know, to travel to the US.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you go over, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you both go over to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: '46, the early part of '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: Early part of '46. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So then I started to sit in the courts. And she went to the various DP camps to help people emigrate to the States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then this happened '46, '47, '48. I remained through 1969. But in '48, all of a sudden there was the trouble with Berlin. The Russians, they wouldn't let the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The blockade.

speaker: interviewee
text: The blockade. So they had what is called the airlift.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time, they thought we were going to get into another war with Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so they alarmed everybody. They said all the women and children have to go back to the States. And they would have to carry only one suitcase and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no car. So I traveled to Bremerhaven with my wife and she took a ship going back to the States. And then after the trouble was over-- by the way, we have a lady here. Her name is Faye-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have to think of the second name. She's 92 years old. Her husband was a colonel and in charge of the airlift. She's a lady here. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So after a year-- no. About a half a year later, the airlift thing didn't bother any more, because at first we thought we were going to get into war. So then my wife came back over and we stayed until 1949. Actually, it was 1950 we came back to the States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to Island Park--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and set up a law practice again.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to spend a little bit more time on your reminiscences as a judge of the International Military Tribunal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was what-- it was officially--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wasn't a member of the International Tribunal. Don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not?

speaker: interviewee
text: No mistake. That was the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The IMT tried the major criminals.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the International Tribunal. They had two judges from each of the four powers. They had eight judges.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now-- and that was-- the first trial was 22 defendants. I attended that trial for a few times.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct. Yeah. Early part of '45 and '46. And the first 22 defendants I visited. I was in the-- in the audience.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were the major war criminals.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. War trial. And at that time, too, I even went to Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of the-- of that major trial, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I saw the defendants. And at first, they were very adamant and they weren't too nervous. But then Telford Taylor--

speaker: interviewer
text: Telford Taylor was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was the presiding US judge, wasn't he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And they showed films of the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every one of these defendants turned away. They couldn't look at the film. They turned away. And they knew that they were guilty. But at the trial, they all blamed Hitler. They said he was the one who we had to follow. And they didn't take that as an answer. And later, when I tried cases after that, I tried judges. I tried people who were-- Aryanized property they took.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the-- do you recall the names of any of the defendants that you tried?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The next trial-- I don't know whether I should give you the name, because that guy is still around. He was a young man then. He was maybe in his late 20s, maybe early 30s. And he became a textile-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know that he still comes to Florida?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't give it to you, because if he knew where I was, it would be dangerous. He took over-- that's the one that I think that signed, 'Heil Hitler.' You must come to Berlin to settle this. You know, if he-- that owner came to Berlin, he would have been executed, that owner. And that letter was introduced at the trial.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you're talking in riddles, because I can't follow this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I know. Well, you asked me the name of that man. And I say, I'm hesitant to give it to you, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, he was a defendant.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the defendant.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he on trial for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the charges against him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Firstly, he was charged with taking over the Aryanized factory of textiles in Berlin. Then, when the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Americans entered Germany and captured it, he went to the factory. And all the textiles, he moved in four vans to Bavaria, put them in warehouses. And I had Property Control, US Property Control, trace that. And they found the textiles. And then he was indicted.

speaker: interviewer
text: For major larceny.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I found him guilty. And I sentenced him to a fine of 5 million Reichsmarks. That was for restitution for the owner. He came back, you know, after the war, and to reclaim his property. And that's when we had the investigation and found that all the textiles were being transported into warehouses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what he was tried for. And I also gave him a five-year prison sentence. And he tried to have--

speaker: interviewer
text: He appealed?

speaker: interviewee
text: He appealed, but he lost. No. They reduced the sentence to two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the time he served already. And later I found out that he was a textile magnate already. And he also got into-- yeah. One day I went with my wife to-- on a trip. I wouldn't go back to Germany, because they would have found me, you know. And when we got to Spain-- we got to Spain and we went to a hotel. And then we see [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a bus. And he had transported-- the bus said-- I'll give you the name-- Neckerman Reisen. His name was Hans Neckerman, Neckerman. And he got into the travel business.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: N-E-C-K-E-R-M-A-N. Hans is H-A-N-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Neckerman, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I saw on the bus Hans Neckerman-- no. Neckerman-Reisen. That means travels.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tours, yes. Travel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Travels. So I got my wife. I said, let's get the hell out of here, because I don't want to see that guy, you know? And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What about other defendants? You mentioned General Vlasov.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well that was the trial of 15 of his regiment. They went to the DP camps, displaced persons camps. And they robbed and they pillaged and they raped and they murdered. And they were all caught.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. This was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's the trials.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Vlasov-- Vlasov's army--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Then Russia asked us to give them Vlasov, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was turned over to them. They executed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the others, all the defendants, even though they escaped and then they were captured, they were all executed. And those were by military police who were shooting. They were all, you know, tied up against a pole, all lined up. And the military police, they shot them. And I was a witness, because I was the presiding judge.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. This was-- they were executed by US Army firing squads, eh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Firing squad. And then, of course, we tried doctors, doctors who emasculated children, judges who were very cruel to Jewish defendants.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of any of these defendants?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- that's so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the doctors?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wouldn't remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever read the book by Robert Jay Lifton?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: That gives the names of all the doctors.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have a book on the-- on the big trials--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --too. Gives all the names, if you want the names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can give you--

speaker: interviewer
text: I thought you might recall some of them yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Of course, you know, some of them escaped the trials. Like Goering, he took poison. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Goering, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of the others, they--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was said that Goering put up quite a brilliant defense.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he took poison.

speaker: interviewee
text: He took poison, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cyanide.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there was one, Hans Frank. Frank.

speaker: interviewer
text: Frank, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He wrote a book with like 28, 30 volumes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Hans Frank was the-- was the-- governor general.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the-- the Gouvernement General in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And he wrote a book with 28 volumes--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --admitting Germany's cruelty and their-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. He made some statement, I think, saying that 1,000 years will not suffice to erase Germany's guilt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right. He said-- yeah. It'll take 1,000 years and Germany wouldn't even--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --be able to live off this cruelty. It was the cruelest part of history in all wars. Never anything like that. Six million Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, you served for three years, then--

speaker: interviewee
text: Four years, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Four years as presiding judge, was that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we tried everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was thieves, even-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a military court, though.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, it was a US military court. Even former counts, they stored weapons and they were caught. And they were tried and convicted and they had to serve time. We didn't execute them. There were thieves. You know, it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your--

speaker: interviewee
text: --right after the war. It was chaos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your court hand down many death sentences, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. On-- on who?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had to be a witness--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --because I was a presiding judge.

speaker: interviewer
text: Apart from the Vlasov--

speaker: interviewee
text: Trial.

speaker: interviewer
text: Vlasov soldiers, who else received death sentences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you see, under the military government [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] law, anybody that carried a gun, a pistol, could get a death sentence, anybody that stole, anybody that committed rape, anybody that did anything to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] defy the government was arrested and prosecuted. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: For subversive activity, you could receive the death sentence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right. They even had one-- I had one trial where there was some defiant Nazi. He was training little boys with guns to fight the Americans that were there. He was arrested and he was tried and given a prison sentence. Anybody that violated any of the government laws, even-- and the German laws, if they-- if one German killed another German, we sent them to the German court.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But if a German killed a Pole or a Pole killed a German, we tried them. Anything that affected the US military government, we tried.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. So you tried die-hard Nazis, then.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Right. I thought the phone was ringing. Yeah. So this went on for four years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many cases did you try altogether, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't even venture a guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: It ran into hundreds, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They run into the hundreds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Looking back on this, do you have any other thoughts?

speaker: interviewee
text: I look back at it like a dream. I couldn't imagine. Sometimes I think, did this really happen to me, a kid on the East Side selling newspapers, that eventually I was going to be a judge and go to the Nuremberg trials? I never even dreamed of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had the rank of full colonel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. As a civilian, though.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't wear a uniform for the court sessions, then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. At the beginning, we used to wear uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we said, well, we want to appear to be democratic, so we took the uniforms off. And then we wore civilian clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: These defendants were-- they had German lawyers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the prosecutors were all from the--

speaker: interviewee
text: American, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: American military.

speaker: interviewee
text: The one trial that I mentioned about Neckerman, it was a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] lieutenant from-- he was from Texas. And he wasn't very fair in his questioning of this defendant. I-- I realized that right away, so I took over the questioning. And of course, he was convicted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the public admitted to the court?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the public admitted to your court?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. It was open.

speaker: interviewer
text: German civilians could come and attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anybody could come in, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it covered by the German press?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the attitude of the public and the press towards these military trials that ran on for three or four years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, look. They knew that these crimes were committed, and they had to pay the consequences.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, that-- that--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, at the beginning, the first defendants that appeared before me, you know, in the US, you say, are you guilty or not guilty? So the interpreter said, 'Schuldig oder nicht schuldig?'

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they answer 'nicht schuldig,' and they start walking towards the door. And the military police, they had to grab them back. They said, you're going to try-- be tried.

speaker: interviewer
text: They thought there was nothing. All they had-- all that was asked of them, are you guilty or not guilty? They said not guilty, but then off they went. And they got caught back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in most cases, they all said, I didn't do anything. It was Hitler. Hitler, he gave the orders.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ein Befehl ist ein Befehl.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: An order is an order. But I-- I assume that the bulk of the defendants pleaded 'nicht schuldig.'

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't think anybody would plead 'schuldig.'

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nobody pleaded guilty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was not in their code. They all said they had a defense, because we didn't do anything. It was Hitler that did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were carrying out orders.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We need to change tapes.
